mnemonics.title: Interrupt Latency Components
mnemonics.content: DID CVI: Detection delay, Instruction completion, Delay due to disabled interrupts, Context saving, Vector fetch, ISR entry overhead

mnemonics.title: Reducing Latency Tips
mnemonics.content: FAST: Fast/short ISRs, Avoid long critical sections, Use DMA, Use proper prioritization/Tail chaining

acronyms.usage: Code executed in response to an interrupt event
Signal generated by hardware to request CPU attention
Hardware module for data transfer without CPU intervention
Highest priority interrupt that cannot be disabled
Architecture with simple instructions for faster execution
Architecture with complex instructions, may have higher latency

visualTips.visualization: Draw a horizontal timeline showing: Interrupt Request → Detection → Context Save → Vector Fetch → ISR Start. Label each segment with approximate cycle counts.
Show a stacked diagram with Main Program at bottom, Low Priority ISR in middle, High Priority ISR on top, demonstrating how preemption adds delay.
Use a traffic signal analogy: Low priority cars (interrupts) block the intersection, preventing high priority emergency vehicles from passing.
Draw CPU registers as boxes being copied to stack memory before ISR execution, emphasizing the cycle overhead.

analogies.analogy: Like a fire alarm going off and the time it takes for the fire department to start driving toward the fire - the alarm is the IRQ, the driving start is ISR execution.
Like saving your work in a document before closing it to answer a phone call - the work must be preserved before switching tasks.
Like a low-priority emergency (minor fire) taking precedence over a major emergency because it arrived first and is blocking resources.
Like finishing one phone call and immediately answering the next ringing phone without putting the first call completely to rest.
Like the longest possible commute during rush hour - includes all possible delays and worst conditions.
